findings.title: Large circular dependency web: 13 files
findings.description:
**Large dependency web:** 13 interconnected files form a strongly connected component.

**Files involved (showing 8/13):**
  • `deserialize.rs`
  • `raw.rs`
  • `url.rs`
  • `chrono.rs`
  • `result.rs`
  • `row.rs`
  • `chrono.rs`
  • `bind_collector.rs`
  ...and 5 more

**Entry points (imported from outside the cycle):**
  • `result.rs` (imported 66 times)
  • `deserialize.rs` (imported 51 times)
  • `connection.rs` (imported 23 times)
  • `chrono.rs` (imported 11 times)
  • `row.rs` (imported 8 times)

**Weakest link to break:** `deserialize.rs` → `raw.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `deserialize.rs` → `raw.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `deserialize.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `raw.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For large cycles:**
1. Extract shared interfaces/types into a new `types.py` or `interfaces.ts`
2. Apply the Dependency Inversion Principle
3. Consider restructuring into layers
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: God class detected: QueryDsl
findings.description:
Class 'QueryDsl' shows signs of being a god class: high method count (26), very large class (1351 LOC).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 26
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 1351

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 26 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=1, p95=2, mean=0.5, n=800
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'QueryDsl' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities
3. **Break down the large class (1351 LOC)**
- Split into smaller, focused classes
- Consider using composition over inheritance
- Extract data classes for complex state

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: High panic density in `clean_tables`: 5 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function `clean_tables` contains 5 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 27, line 28, line 29, line 30, line 31. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 83).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.
